<report version="1">
  <run_echo>
    <run_id>chaassupport-prompt-review-debug-ring0-20260306</run_id>
    <brief_hash>mb-debug-ring0-review-v1</brief_hash>
    <attempt_count>1</attempt_count>
  </run_echo>
  <status>completed</status>
  <summary>The misroute is primarily caused by ring-oriented telemetry heuristics firing before intent is resolved: the prompt classifies any mention of “rings” as Investigate (`extensions\CHAASSupport.yml:20`), the Database extension auto-activates on `ring` (`src\Extensions\CHAASSupport\Database.cs:14`), and the Investigate workflow immediately goes “Telemetry first” with ring-based Kusto search when no IDs exist (`extensions\CHAASSupport.yml:68-76`). Eval evidence matches this: `CHAASSupport.investigate-debug-ring0` scores 0/25 and the trace shows immediate `CHAASSupport_Database` activation plus a blind `IceEvent | where deploy_ring == "Ring0"` query (`eschat-eval-results.json:6-11`, `:21-24`, `:118-141`), while strong telemetry-led cases like `investigate-job-id-lookup` and `investigate-resource-exception-with-correlation-ids` remain strong (`eschat-eval-results.json:2736-2739`, `:7829-7832`).</summary>
  <findings>
    <root_causes_for_debug_ring0_failure>
      <item>Prompt mode selection is too broad: “rings” is listed as a generic telemetry clue for Investigate, even when ring is only contextual and the real ask is lifecycle/behavior (“do we delete files / not create them?”). See `extensions\CHAASSupport.yml:19-21`.</item>
      <item>Tool activation is even broader than the prompt: Database auto-triggers on `ring` regardless of whether the question is about live runtime evidence or source behavior. See `src\Extensions\CHAASSupport\Database.cs:14`.</item>
      <item>Investigate workflow step 1 has no guardrail for “implementation question with ops vocabulary”; once Investigate is chosen, it directs “no IDs but scoped clues present -> query ... with ring ...” which is exactly the wrong behavior for debug-file lifecycle asks. See `extensions\CHAASSupport.yml:68-76`.</item>
      <item>Eval trace confirms the failure mode: the agent activates only `CHAASSupport_Database`, fetches Kusto sources, then runs `IceEvent` filtered solely by `deploy_ring == "Ring0"` and recent errors, producing unrelated live failures rather than answering the file-lifecycle question. See `eschat-eval-results.json:21-24`, `:118-141`, `:152-160`.</item>
    </root_causes_for_debug_ring0_failure>
    <high_leverage_changes>
      <item><rank>1</rank><type>both</type><change>Remove `ring` from the Database `[On(...)]` trigger and add a prompt rule that ring/version/scale-unit mentions are only telemetry-routing signals when paired with live-failure evidence (job_id, correlation_id, recent error text, concrete timeframe/scope). This is the cleanest fix because it removes the premature extension activation and then teaches the prompt the right distinction. Files: `src\Extensions\CHAASSupport\Database.cs:14`, `extensions\CHAASSupport.yml:19-21`, `:68-76`.</change></item>
      <item><rank>2</rank><type>prompt-only</type><change>Strengthen Q&A routing with explicit negative examples: questions about file lifecycle, creation/deletion policy, implementation behavior, config, repo/module/function/class should always start with `CHAASSupport_Code`, even if they mention Ring0/versions. Best place: expand `extensions\CHAASSupport.yml:50-54` and reinforce `:133`.</change></item>
      <item><rank>3</rank><type>prompt-only</type><change>Add a pre-Kusto gate in Investigate step 1: “Before telemetry, decide whether the user is asking about an active incident or how the system works.” Only run Kusto first for active/runtime questions with live evidence; otherwise jump to code/docs/wiki first and use telemetry only if code/docs leave an unresolved runtime question. Best place: `extensions\CHAASSupport.yml:68-76`.</change></item>
    </high_leverage_changes>
    <likely_side_effects_on_currently_good_scenarios>
      <item>Telemetry-first scenarios with hard runtime clues should be preserved: `investigate-job-id-lookup` stays safe because `job`/`job_id` still trigger Database and the workflow already prefers `job_id` first (`src\Extensions\CHAASSupport\Database.cs:14`, `extensions\CHAASSupport.yml:71`; eval strong at `eschat-eval-results.json:2736-2739`).</item>
      <item>Correlation-id / exception investigations also stay safe because `error`, `exception`, `correlation_id`, `incident`, `timeout`, etc. remain strong DB triggers; `investigate-resource-exception-with-correlation-ids` is already high-scoring (`eschat-eval-results.json:7829-7832`).</item>
      <item>The main risk is slightly reducing sensitivity for vague fleet asks that only say “Ring0 issue” without any error text or timeframe. That is acceptable and should be mitigated in prompt wording by telling the model to ask for one concrete runtime clue before Kusto rather than guessing.</item>
      <item>Do not weaken the existing “Kusto never for source code/config” rule; it is already correct (`extensions\CHAASSupport.yml:133`) but currently loses to earlier mode/trigger heuristics.</item>
    </likely_side_effects_on_currently_good_scenarios>
  </findings>
  <recommendations>
    <item>
      <rank>1</rank>
      <type>both</type>
      <recommendation>Delete `ring` from `CHAASSupport_Database`’s `[On(...)]` trigger and add an explicit prompt rule that ring/version/tenant/scale-unit only justify Kusto when paired with live-failure evidence. This is the highest-leverage change because it fixes both the automatic misfire and the prompt’s current overclassification.</recommendation>
      <rationale>`Database.cs:14` is the mechanical misrouter; `CHAASSupport.yml:20` and `:73` are the conceptual misrouters. Fixing both sharply improves debug-ring0-like asks without touching strong job-id/correlation-id flows.</rationale>
    </item>
    <item>
      <rank>2</rank>
      <type>prompt-only</type>
      <recommendation>Rewrite the Q&A tool-selection bullets to explicitly include “file lifecycle / create-delete behavior / debug artifact policy / implementation behavior” under `CHAASSupport_Code`, and say “NEVER use Kusto for these even if Ring0/version is mentioned.”</recommendation>
      <rationale>The current mapping says “code/config/implementation/module/file/function/class/repo” (`CHAASSupport.yml:50-54`) but does not name the exact class of failure seen in debug-ring0, so the model falls back to ops language instead of behavior language.</rationale>
    </item>
    <item>
      <rank>3</rank>
      <type>prompt-only</type>
      <recommendation>Add an explicit intent gate ahead of Investigate step 1: “Is the user asking about a live incident, or asking how the system behaves?” If the latter, search code/docs/wiki first; use Kusto only to confirm a runtime hypothesis after that.</recommendation>
      <rationale>This preserves successful telemetry-first incident handling while preventing blind ring-based searches like the one shown in `eschat-eval-results.json:118-141`.</rationale>
    </item>
  </recommendations>
  <risks>
    <item>If `ring` is removed from the DB trigger without prompt compensation, very underspecified fleet issues may require one extra clarification before Kusto.</item>
    <item>If the prompt change is too aggressive and says “ring implies Q&A unless IDs exist,” it could under-route legitimate live incidents that have error text + ring but no IDs. Keep the gate keyed to “live runtime evidence,” not strictly IDs.</item>
    <item>A prompt-only fix helps, but extension auto-triggering may still overpower it in some runs; that is why the combined fix is preferable.</item>
  </risks>
  <next_steps>
    <item>Implement recommendation #1 first: `Database.cs:14`, then `CHAASSupport.yml:19-21`, `:50-54`, and `:68-76`.</item>
    <item>Re-run only the targeted investigation eval slice: `investigate-debug-ring0`, plus guardrail scenarios `investigate-job-id-lookup` and `investigate-resource-exception-with-correlation-ids`.</item>
    <item>If debug-ring0 improves but vague live ring incidents regress, add one small prompt fallback: ask for a concrete error/timestamp when ring is the only runtime clue.</item>
  </next_steps>
</report>

I reviewed the prompt + CHAASSupport extensions with a focus on `investigate-debug-ring0` and similar cases. Highest-leverage finding: we need a **new branch inside Investigate** for **static/conceptual investigation questions** like file lifecycle, location, creation/deletion, retention, and config behavior — these should go to **Code/Wiki/EngHub first**, not Kusto. Right now the prompt sends almost any “error + Ring0/job” phrasing into telemetry-first, and `Database.cs` reinforces that with a broad `[On(...)]` trigger.

### Top 3 changes to discuss
1. **Prompt-only:** Add an “Investigate but static/conceptual” gate before **Telemetry first**  
   - If the user is asking **how/when/whether/location/configuration** of debug files/logs/artifacts, route to **CHAASSupport_Code / Wiki / EngHub first**
   - Only use Kusto first when there are **concrete runtime clues** like `job_id`, `correlation_id`, explicit live incident wording, or runtime failure signatures

2. **Extension-code:** Tighten `Database.cs` activation  
   - The broad trigger on `error`, `job`, and `ring` is a big reason `investigate-debug-ring0` misroutes
   - We should keep strong telemetry anchors (`job_id`, `correlation_id`, incident/IcM terms, explicit exception signatures), but stop letting ambiguous words alone activate Kusto

3. **Both:** Make non-Kusto sources explicitly advertise lifecycle/log/debug-artifact behavior  
   - Add wording to the prompt/tool descriptions that these sources answer **debug/log file lifecycle, retention, creation, deletion, and location** questions

### Main risk
If we make the carveout too broad, we could weaken strong telemetry-first scenarios like:
- `investigate-job-id-lookup`
- `investigate-resource-exception-with-correlation-ids`

So the safe boundary is:
- **Conceptual/static behavior question** → code/wiki/docs first
- **Concrete live run/incident question** → telemetry first

My recommendation is to implement **#1 first**, then the **smallest safe version of #2**.  
**Focused question:** do you want the carveout to cover only **debug/log/artifact lifecycle/location** questions, or also broader “how does this behavior work in prod?” investigative asks?

response:
### Regression Check: Previous Council Findings vs. Current Artifacts

| # | Original Finding | Status | Evidence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Approach B: Contract-driven** | ✅ FIXED | `forge-gpt.agent.md` explicitly defines the `Mission Brief` -> `Dispatch` -> `REPORT` cycle. The agent cannot act without a written contract (Brief). |
| 2 | **Zero-personality coordinator** | ✅ FIXED | System prompt commands strict, robotic neutrality. No conversational filler is allowed in the `DISPATCH` lane. |
| 3 | **Lane locking** | ✅ FIXED | `SKILL.md` forces a mutually exclusive choice: `T1_ANSWER` (chat), `DISPATCH` (work), or `BLOCKED` (stop) before any tool use. |
| 4 | **Top-loaded constraints** | ✅ FIXED | Constraints (`NO_EDIT`, `NO_BUILD`, `DISPATCH_ATOMIC`) are placed at the very top of `forge-gpt.agent.md` for maximum context window salience. |
| 5 | **Separate terminal tokens** | 🔄 PARTIALLY | The logic exists, but the specific tokens for sub-agent status vs. coordinator status need rigorous integration testing to ensure no leakage. |
| 6 | **Validate REPORT** | ✅ FIXED | The workflow explicitly requires the coordinator to validate the `report.v1` artifact from the sub-agent before marking the task `DISPATCH_COMPLETE`. |
| 7 | **Schema-first design** | ✅ FIXED | `schemas/mission-brief.v1.md` and `schemas/report.v1.md` exist and are referenced in the build script and agent prompts. |
| 8 | **Self-contained artifacts** | 🔄 PARTIALLY | The artifacts are designed to be self-contained, but without runtime evidence, we cannot verify if the agent correctly populates the `run_id` and links back to the parent session. |

---

### New Findings: Deep Evaluation & Fit Analysis

#### 1. Workflow Fit: High Alignment, High Friction Risk
The non-forge session history shows a user who values **verification** and **auditability** (e.g., "verify the changes," "ask experts"). The current assistants often fail by "collapsing state"—jumping from problem statement directly to "Done, fixed it" without visible intermediate steps, leading to false confidence (e.g., the negative nights bug).

*   **Fit:** `forge-gpt` is a strong architectural fit because it forces the **intermediate state** (Mission Brief) to be visible. It prevents the "collapse" by making the plan a distinct artifact before execution.
*   **Risk:** The user frequently uses pressure prompts ("Just fix it," "Stop delegating"). `forge-gpt`'s strict refusal to edit files directly (`NO_EDIT` constraint) directly contradicts the user's explicit instructions in those moments.
    *   *Mitigation:* The prompt explicitly reinterprets "Just fix it" as "Immediate Dispatch," but the user may initially perceive this as disobedience if the dispatch process is slow.

#### 2. The "Silent Failure" Risk in Sub-Agent Handoffs
The architecture relies heavily on `forge-execute-gpt` and `forge-verify-gpt`. There is **zero evidence** in the session history of these sub-agents being tested.
*   **Issue:** If the coordinator (Forge-GPT) creates a Mission Brief, but the executor (Forge-Execute-GPT) is hallucinations-prone or fails to write a valid `report.v1`, the coordinator has no defined recovery path in the current artifacts. It effectively "hangs" waiting for a report that never validates.
*   **Missing Artifact:** A `rescue` or `retry` protocol for failed dispatches is not evident in the high-level descriptions.

#### 3. Verification Gap
In session `0916192a` (negative nights), the assistant claimed a fix that was incomplete. `forge-gpt` delegates verification to `forge-verify-gpt`.
*   **Critical Question:** Does `forge-verify-gpt` have access to the *original* user complaint, or only the `Mission Brief`?
*   **Risk:** If the Mission Brief is lossy (summarizing "fix bug" but missing "handle empty petName"), the verifier will verify the *Brief* was met, but the *User's Issue* remains unfixed. The artifacts suggests the Brief is the source of truth, creating a potential "Telephone Game" failure mode.

#### 4. Missing Evidence & Next Steps
We have **no runtime evidence** of:
1.  The coordinator correctly parsing a complex user request into a granular Mission Brief.
2.  The coordinator resisting the urge to "help" by writing code when the user gets angry.
3.  The sub-agents correctly writing to the `forge_runs` and `forge_effects` tables.

**Immediate Next Steps:**
1.  **Synthetic Stress Test:** Run a session specifically using the "Pressure Prompt" (`Just fix the bug, stop talking`) to see if `forge-gpt` collapses or correctly dispatches.
2.  **Telemetry Check:** Verify the `forge_runs` table schema is actually deployed to the session DB.
3.  **Verification Loop Audit:** Review `modes\verify.md` to ensure it explicitly references the *original user request* alongside the Mission Brief to prevent context loss.

#### Minimal Failing Eval Case (Hypothetical)
*   **Scenario:** User says, "The login page crashes on mobile. Fix it immediately, do not plan."
*   **Current Likely Failure:** `forge-gpt` enters `T1_ANSWER` mode to apologize or explain why it needs to plan, violating the user's "do not plan" constraint, OR it tries to edit `login.tsx` directly, violating its own `NO_EDIT` constraint.
*   **Success Criteria:** `forge-gpt` silently enters `DISPATCH` mode, creates a `Mission Brief` (treating "Fix it" as the plan), and dispatches to `forge-execute-gpt` without user-facing friction.

response:
Below is the skeptical re-review; the short version is: **the design/build work substantially implements the intended contract model, but almost all behavior claims are still prompt-level and unproven in real runtime use.**

## 1. Regression Check

| # | Original Finding | Status | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Approach B: contract-driven GPT fork | 🔄 PARTIALLY FIXED | Present in `agents\forge-gpt\forge-gpt.agent.md:21-31`, `agents\forge-gpt\SKILL.md:77-98`, and the schema files; `build-forge-gpt-plugin.ps1:176-232` packages a standalone `forge-gpt` plugin. But the only session evidence (`a332...`, `f6f...`) is design/build, not the installed agent handling independent user work. |
| 2 | Zero-personality coordinator | 🔄 PARTIALLY FIXED | Explicitly stated in `forge-gpt.agent.md:57-67` and reinforced by “dispatch engine, not a coding partner” in `SKILL.md:41-45`. No runtime transcript shows the agent actually staying cold/terse under pressure or after dispatch. |
| 3 | Lane locking before any tool call | 🔄 PARTIALLY FIXED | Strongly encoded in `SKILL.md:10-19` plus mandatory one-line classification preamble in `SKILL.md:43-49`. But there is **no transcript** showing `T1_ANSWER / DISPATCH / BLOCKED` being chosen and then obeyed before tool use. |
| 4 | Top-loaded constraints for GPT salience | 🔄 PARTIALLY FIXED | Good salience structure: first-action rule in `forge-gpt.agent.md:11-19`, lane lock and hard constraints at top of `SKILL.md:10-28`, and mode constraints top-loaded in `modes\execute.md:10-19` / `modes\verify.md:10-17`. Still prompt-only; no empirical proof GPT actually obeys these under real load. |
| 5 | Separate coordinator terminal tokens from subagent status values | 🔄 PARTIALLY FIXED | Implemented clearly: subagents forbid `DISPATCH_COMPLETE` (`execute.md:16`, `verify.md:14`), coordinator owns it (`SKILL.md:25-27, 130-168`), and `report.v1.md:14-18` separates status enum from terminal token. No runtime evidence of a malformed subagent report being rejected correctly. |
| 6 | Validate REPORT before DISPATCH_COMPLETE | 🔄 PARTIALLY FIXED | This is specified in detail in `SKILL.md:130-168` and `report.v1.md:150-165`. But there is no installed-agent transcript showing REPORT parse/echo/status/evidence validation actually happening before completion is announced. |
| 7 | Schema-first layered artifact design | ✅ FIXED / EVIDENCED | Fully materialized: `schemas\mission-brief.v1.md`, `schemas\report.v1.md`, coordinator + execute + verify artifacts, and build wiring in `build-forge-gpt-plugin.ps1`. This is one of the few findings that is genuinely evidenced by files/build output rather than hoped-for runtime behavior. |
| 8 | Keep GPT artifacts self-contained but traceable to Forge | ✅ FIXED / EVIDENCED | Self-contained bundle is produced in `dist-forge-gpt`; traceability is explicit via lineage comments in the GPT artifacts and shared Forge mode reuse in `build-forge-gpt-plugin.ps1:209-274`. Caveat: the build script itself notes shared mode names are still reused rather than fully namespaced, so isolation is good but not perfect. |

## 2. New Findings

### What the transcripts really say about forge-gpt today
- **What is proven:** a serious design/build effort happened on 2026-03-06 (`f6f...`, `a332...`), producing docs, prompt artifacts, schemas, a build script, and a successful local plugin install.
- **What is not proven:** there is **no post-install product-forge session** showing forge-gpt taking a normal user request, classifying, dispatching, validating a REPORT, and stopping correctly.
- **Bottom line:** current evidence supports **“artifact implemented”**, not **“agent behavior validated.”**

### Fit to the user’s observed workflow
- **Strong fit areas**
  - The user repeatedly works in a **plan → proceed → verify** cadence; four sessions explicitly contain the `proceed` then `verify the changes` loop, and forge-gpt is architected around exactly that separation.
  - The user also likes **audit/review → expert second opinion → prioritized implementation** (`c500...`), which fits the coordinator/subagent model well.
  - The user pressure-tests operating discipline with prompts like `DO it yourself, stop delegating...`; forge-gpt explicitly encodes “pressure still means dispatch,” which is the right product stance.
- **Weak fit areas**
  - The dataset is dominated by **one-turn sessions (171/201)** and terse operational asks; a heavy coordinator can feel slow or ceremonial unless it responds with very compact, decisive summaries.
  - The user often wants **visible finality fast** (“All 7 bugs fixed. 10/10 tests passing.”); if forge-gpt emits too much routing/meta chatter, it will feel worse than baseline even if architecturally cleaner.

### Instruction-following outlook: promising on paper, unproven in practice
- **Phase discipline:** likely improved vs baseline because planning, execution, and verification are explicitly separated into different modes; however this is still only prompt architecture.
- **Verification discipline:** the strongest part of the design; REPORT validation, verify mode, and coordinator stop rules directly target the baseline system’s habit of overclaiming completion.
- **Pressure-prompt discipline:** explicitly addressed and likely improved, but again there is no real transcript showing the installed agent resisting `stop delegating` and still behaving correctly.

### Net-new issues / risks not covered by the previous findings

1. **No empirical runtime proof at all**
   - Evidence: searches found only design/build sessions; there are **0 post-install product-forge sessions** after `a332...` turn 5 where forge-gpt handled a real task.
   - Risk: the entire review can overrate prompt architecture because there is no live behavioral sample.
   - Minimal failing eval:  
     **Prompt:** `Fix BUG-002 in src/services/bookings.js where totalPrice uses nights+1 instead of nights.`  
     **Fail today:** no evidence it lane-locks, dispatches execute-gpt, validates a REPORT, and stops.  
     **Pass after fix/validation:** transcript shows exact DISPATCH path with no coordinator edits/builds.

2. **Baseline failure mode = premature success claims; forge-gpt is designed to fix it, but that fix is not yet demonstrated**
   - Evidence: `0916192a...` initially claimed validation was fixed, then the user caught a missing `petName` case; `bbafdc45...` collapsed scoping into “Done. Notification system is live.” and then ignored the later request to plan.
   - Risk: GPT historically overclaims completion in this repo; unless forge-gpt is tested, the same failure may survive under a nicer contract wrapper.
   - Minimal failing eval:  
     **Prompt sequence:** `Create a plan to fix P0, then P1, then P2.` → `proceed` → `verify the changes`  
     **Fail today:** coordinator or worker skips plan boundary, jumps to implementation, or self-verifies without separate verify mode.  
     **Pass after fix:** three distinct phases with separate outputs and verify-gpt evidence.

3. **Run ledger / idempotency / resume behavior is specified but completely untested**
   - Evidence: `SKILL.md:99-128` defines `forge_runs` / `forge_effects`, but there is no transcript showing those tables created or reused; session `2a8d5c14...` exists as a duplicate start with `NULL` assistant output, which is exactly the kind of messy continuity case this system claims to handle.
   - Risk: retries/resumes may duplicate side effects or lose state.
   - Minimal failing eval:  
     **Scenario:** dispatch an implementation, interrupt after the subagent returns, then resume with `continue`.  
     **Fail today:** new `run_id`, duplicated side effects, or invented state.  
     **Pass after fix:** same `run_id`, incremented `attempt_count`, no repeated bookkeeping/effects.

4. **Packaging isolation is incomplete because shared mode names are still reused**
   - Evidence: `build-forge-gpt-plugin.ps1:13-15` explicitly says the bundle “intentionally reuses shared forge-* skill names” and treats full namespacing as future work.
   - Risk: if both Forge and forge-gpt are installed, shared-mode resolution/identity may drift or confuse debugging; this weakens the “self-contained fork” story operationally.
   - Minimal failing eval:  
     **Scenario:** with both plugins installed, ask forge-gpt to do an explore-first task.  
     **Fail today:** the runtime loads a shared `forge-plan`/`forge-explore` behavior that is not obviously the packaged GPT-tuned copy.  
     **Pass after fix:** all GPT-path skills are namespaced or unambiguously resolved from the forge-gpt bundle.

5. **There is no forge-gpt-specific eval harness wired to the new contracts**
   - Evidence: the repo has `agents\forge\evals\run-evals.py`, but nothing equivalent under `agents\forge-gpt\`; searches found no forge-gpt eval files.
   - Risk: prompt regressions will be detected only anecdotally, which is especially dangerous for GPT where salience/obedience can drift.
   - Minimal failing eval:  
     **Case:** worker returns freeform Markdown plus a valid-looking summary.  
     **Fail today:** no automated guard proves coordinator blocks it.  
     **Pass after fix:** eval mechanically verifies BLOCKED on malformed/non-schema report.

6. **Coordinator may still be too verbose/meta for the user’s dominant workflow**
   - Evidence: most product-forge sessions are one-turn asks, and the user often wants immediate action under pressure; forge-gpt adds mandatory skill invocation, lane classification, Mission Brief construction, and serial dispatch.
   - Risk: even if correct, it may feel slower and less satisfying than the baseline for routine bugfixes.
   - Minimal failing eval:  
     **Prompt:** `Just fix the failing test in the pricing module, stop asking questions.`  
     **Fail today:** coordinator emits a long classification/contract preamble or asks for scope that should be recoverable from context.  
     **Pass after fix:** very short decisive response, direct dispatch, terse completion summary.

### Missing evidence before trust is warranted
- A real installed-agent transcript for a **simple one-turn bug fix**.
- A real installed-agent transcript for **plan → proceed → verify**.
- A real installed-agent transcript under a **pressure prompt** (`stop delegating`, `just fix it`).
- At least one **malformed REPORT rejection** case.
- At least one **resume/retry** case proving `run_id` and `attempt_count` behavior.

response:
## 1. **Regression Check**

| # | Original Finding | Status (✅/❌/🔄/🆕) | Expert Agreement | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Approach B: contract-driven GPT fork | 🔄 | Unanimous partial | All three agree the contract model is materially built (`Mission Brief -> Dispatch -> REPORT`, standalone plugin build/install), but there is still no real post-install transcript proving the installed agent executes independent user tasks correctly. |
| 2 | Zero-personality coordinator | 🔄 | Majority partial (2/3) | Prompt artifacts clearly instruct cold/neutral coordinator behavior, but only one expert treated that as effectively fixed; the other two correctly note there is no runtime evidence that the agent stays terse and non-chatty under pressure. |
| 3 | Lane locking before any tool call | 🔄 | Majority partial (2/3) | Lane lock is strongly encoded in artifacts (`T1_ANSWER / DISPATCH / BLOCKED` before tools), but no transcript shows the installed agent actually classifying first and obeying the lock during live task execution. |
| 4 | Top-loaded constraints for GPT salience | 🔄 | Majority partial (2/3) | Constraints are visibly moved toward the top of `forge-gpt.agent.md` / `SKILL.md`, but two experts note this remains prompt-level, and one flags that some critical rules are still not top-most enough for maximum salience. |
| 5 | Separate coordinator terminal tokens from subagent status values | 🔄 | Majority partial (2/3) | Artifact design separates coordinator terminal tokens from subagent report status, but there is no runtime proof that malformed or leaking subagent outputs are rejected correctly in practice. |
| 6 | Validate REPORT before DISPATCH_COMPLETE | 🔄 | Majority partial (2/3) | Validation protocol exists and is much stronger than before, but experts converge that it is still unproven at runtime; one also flags wording weakness (“should” vs “must”) that could dilute GPT obedience. |
| 7 | Schema-first layered artifact design | ✅ | Majority fixed (2/3) | Schemas, layered artifacts, coordinator/execute/verify modes, and build wiring are all present. One expert dissents because some schema terms remain vague/undefined, but the implementation direction is clearly realized. |
| 8 | Keep GPT artifacts self-contained but traceable to Forge | ✅ | Majority fixed (2/3) | Standalone `dist-forge-gpt`, separate manifest/name, lineage to shared Forge modes, and successful local install all support this. The dissent is that runtime traceability fields (`run_id`, linkage) are not yet empirically verified. |

---

## 2. **Majority View**

| # | Majority Position | Dissenting View | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `forge-gpt` is **architecturally improved but not behaviorally proven**; prompt success is not runtime success. | One expert was more willing to mark several behavior items as fixed based on artifact quality alone. | P0 |
| 2 | Zero-personality behavior is **specified, not demonstrated**. | One expert treated the coordinator’s neutrality as effectively fixed from prompt design. | P1 |
| 3 | Lane locking is **implemented in artifacts but unproven in live execution**. | One expert marked lane locking fixed because the artifact rule is explicit and first-positioned. | P1 |
| 4 | Top-loaded salience is **better but not conclusively sufficient** for GPT obedience under pressure. | One expert viewed current prompt placement as fully adequate. | P1 |
| 5 | REPORT validation is **meaningfully designed but still lacks runtime proof**. | One expert treated the validation protocol as fixed because the structure is explicit. | P1 |
| 6 | Schema-first design is **substantially delivered**. | One expert argued it remains partial because some schema terms/rubrics are underspecified. | P2 |
| 7 | Self-contained + traceable packaging is **substantially delivered**. | One expert argued it is only partial until runtime lineage fields are exercised and proven. | P2 |

---

## 3. **Unique Insights**

| # | Unique Insight | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Verification gap via lossy Mission Brief**: verify may end up validating the brief instead of the original user complaint, letting a structurally valid but semantically wrong outcome pass. | P0 |
| 2 | **Silent sub-agent handoff failure**: if executor/verify fails to produce a valid `report.v1`, recovery behavior is unclear; this is a coordinator reliability hole. | P0 |
| 3 | **Packaging isolation risk**: shared mode-name reuse may create incomplete isolation between Forge and forge-gpt despite separate plugin packaging. | P1 |
| 4 | **Schema ambiguity risk**: undefined terms like `R0-R4`, vague evidence/materiality rules, and unclear idempotency semantics create hallucination surface inside the protocol itself. | P1 |
| 5 | **Normative wording weakness**: “should validate” instead of “must validate” weakens instruction force right at the most safety-critical checkpoint. | P1 |
| 6 | **Plugin coexistence risk**: no explicit conflict detection or coexistence testing when both Forge and forge-gpt are installed. | P2 |
| 7 | **Model pin fragility**: heavy dependence on `gpt-5.4` could make behavior brittle across model/version shifts. | P2 |

---

## 4. **Conflicts**

1. **How much prompt implementation counts as “fixed.”**  
   - **Position A:** Several items are effectively fixed because the artifact structure is explicit and strong.  
   - **Position B/C:** Most behavior findings remain partial until shown in live transcripts.  
   - **Supported by evidence:** Sparse empirical evidence; only two relevant sessions exist, both design/build sessions, not installed-agent runtime sessions.

2. **Schema-first status.**  
   - **Position A/B:** Fixed enough to mark complete because schema files, layered artifacts, and build wiring exist.  
   - **Position C:** Only partial because key terms and evidence semantics remain underspecified.  
   - **Supported by evidence:** Artifact existence supports A/B; schema ambiguity examples support C.

3. **Self-contained but traceable status.**  
   - **Position B/C:** Fixed because packaging is standalone and lineage is explicit.  
   - **Position A:** Partial because runtime traceability fields and linkage behavior are still unproven.  
   - **Supported by evidence:** Build/install success supports B/C; absence of runtime ledger/linkage evidence supports A.

4. **Lane locking and top-loaded constraints.**  
   - **Position A:** Fixed in practice from prompt architecture.  
   - **Position B/C:** Only partial because no live transcript shows the installed agent obeying them before tools under user pressure.  
   - **Supported by evidence:** Artifact layout supports A; missing runtime transcript supports B/C.

---

## 5. **Recommendation**

Treat `forge-gpt` as a **promising but unproven coordinator fork**: the design is directionally right, the contract model is materially implemented, and it is better aligned to this user’s need for visible phases, auditability, and verification than the baseline assistant. But do **not** treat it as production-ready until it survives a targeted eval suite against the user’s actual workflow—especially repeated benchmark prompts, pressure prompts, plan→proceed→verify flows, malformed reports, and interruption/resume cases—because the dominant historical failure mode is not lack of structure, it is **premature completeness claims and phase collapse under pressure**.

| # | Action | Severity | Effort | Blocks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Build a **forge-gpt eval harness** using the user’s repeated real prompts (booking bug, notification ask, bounded-context question, branch/commit query, off-by-one bug) and score lane choice, phase obedience, evidence quality, and overclaim rate. | P0 | M | None |

| 6 | Define all ambiguous schema terms (`R0-R4`, materiality, evidence thresholds, idempotency scope, bridge semantics) in the schema docs. | P1 | S | None |
| 7 | Add a **malformed-report rejection test** to prove subagent/coordinator token separation and no leakage of coordinator terminal states from subagents. | P1 | S | Eval harness |
| 8 | Add a **resume/interruption test** for run ledger / idempotency / restart behavior. | P1 | M | Eval harness |
| 9 | Benchmark **verbosity and latency** on one-turn sessions; widen `T1_ANSWER` for read-only/simple tasks so forge-gpt does not impose coordinator ceremony where the user expects instant answers. | P1 | M | Eval harness |
| 10 | Add explicit **PACKAGING/COEXISTENCE tests** for Forge + forge-gpt installed together, including shared mode-name collision checks. | P2 | M | None |
| 11 | Validate runtime **lineage fields** (`run_id`, brief/report linkage, ledger writes) in an end-to-end smoke test. | P2 | M | Eval harness |
| 12 | Add a **model-variance smoke test** or reduce brittle model-specific assumptions so behavior does not depend too heavily on a single GPT version. | P2 | M | Eval harness |

**Decision**: Choose **Option 3: hybrid layered harness**. **Principle**: preserve the proven runtime-facing/event-grading foundation while adding explicit contracts around scenarios, results, and forge-gpt-specific semantics. **Alternative rejected**: a net-new runner, because v1 needs installed-plugin fidelity fast, and the repo already has the hard-won session/runtime machinery.

| Option | Pros | Cons | Complexity / Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Extend `run-evals.py` / `run-loops.py` directly** | Fastest path; highest reuse of session discovery, sandboxing, `events.jsonl` grading, resume/interactive execution, flake fixes already learned; easiest to get first post-install forge-gpt transcripts | Risks accreting more one-off flags into Forge-specific runners; weak scenario contract/data model; result artifacts stay runner-shaped rather than harness-shaped; harder to express REPORT-schema and complaint-semantic checks cleanly | **Low complexity / Medium long-term risk** |
| **2. New ESChat-style runner around installed forge-gpt plugin** | Clean scenario-as-data design; clean separation of execution/judging/scoring; easier future extensibility | Rebuilds the hardest parts already solved here: Copilot session capture, resume quirks, phantom-turn handling, sandbox rules, result stability; highest schedule risk; likely lower fidelity during v1 because time goes into plumbing, not behavior coverage | **High complexity / High delivery risk** — **do not pursue for v1** |
| **3. Hybrid layered harness on top of existing runners** | Best v1 fit: keep real installed-plugin runtime path (`copilot --agent ...`, session-state, sandboxes), but add a new **scenario schema**, **runtime adapter**, **forge-gpt contract checks**, **durable result schema**, and **semantic judge**; directly targets pressure resistance, malformed REPORT, semantic verify, resume/idempotency, and loop quality without discarding proven mechanics | Some refactor needed to extract shared execution/parsing code from the two scripts; slightly slower than option 1 | **Medium complexity / Low-medium risk** — **recommended** |

**Recommended v1 shape:** keep the existing execution/parsing core as the **runtime adapter**; add a single forge-gpt eval package with (a) **scenario-as-data** files for single-turn and multi-turn installed-plugin sessions, (b) **mechanical graders** for lane lock / skill line / Mission Brief / REPORT parse / run_id-brief_hash-attempt echo / DISPATCH_COMPLETE discipline, (c) **semantic judge** that compares behavior to the **original complaint + expected acceptance**, not just final text, (d) **separate evidence channels** for process trace (`events.jsonl`, task prompts, assistant turns, REPORT block, ledger/sql signals) vs final-answer evidence, and (e) a **deterministic result artifact** per run with scenario metadata, transcript pointers, grader outputs, semantic verdicts, and aggregate score. **Concrete suites for v1:** 1) pressure-prompt resistance single-turns, 2) malformed REPORT cases, 3) verify-against-original-complaint loops, 4) resume/idempotency loops with reused `run_id`/`attempt_count`, 5) plan→proceed→verify loops that require valid Mission Brief and REPORT transitions; practically, this means extracting shared code from `run-evals.py`/`run-loops.py` into common runtime modules, introducing `forge-gpt` scenario/result schemas first, then authoring a small high-signal corpus before any broader harness rewrite.

response:
The expert recommendation for the `forge-gpt` v1 evaluation harness is **Option 3: Hybrid Layered Harness**. This approach preserves the robust sandbox/git infrastructure from the existing system while introducing a new `Scenario Runner` specifically designed for `forge-gpt`'s strict state machine and contract-driven behavior.

### 1. Executive Summary

**Recommendation:** Build a **Hybrid Layered Harness** (`agents/forge-gpt/evals/`) that reuses the low-level sandbox/git primitives from `agents/forge/evals/` but implements a new high-level **Scenario Runner**.

*   **Why:** `forge-gpt` is a coordinator with a strict state machine (Lock → Dispatch → Report → Unlock). The existing `run-evals.py` is tool-count focused and cannot effectively validate these state transitions, contract adherence, or malformed report handling without becoming unmaintainable.
*   **Key Feature:** A new **Scenario-as-Data** format that explicitly models the multi-turn conversation, expected state transitions, and contract validations (e.g., "Did it reject the malformed report?").

---

### 2. Candidate Analysis

#### Option 1: Extend Existing `run-evals.py`
Modify the current script to support `forge-gpt` via flags and new test case fields.
*   **Pros:** Lowest effort; reuses all existing code.
*   **Cons:** High risk of "testing the wrong thing." The existing harness counts tool calls (dispatch, edit) but doesn't validate the *content* of the Mission Brief or the *response* to a Report. `forge-gpt`'s value is in its contract enforcement, which this harness would largely ignore.
*   **Verdict:** **Reject.** Too coarse-grained for a coordinator agent.

#### Option 2: New ESChat-Inspired Runner (Greenfield)
Build a completely new runner from scratch, including sandbox management, based on external inspiration.
*   **Pros:** tailored perfectly to `forge-gpt`.
*   **Cons:** High effort; reinvents solved problems (git worktrees, cleanup, efficient resetting).
*   **Verdict:** **Reject.** Unnecessary duplication of infrastructure.

#### Option 3: Hybrid Layered Harness (Recommended)
Refactor `run-evals.py` to extract shared primitives, then build a specialized `forge-gpt` runner on top.
*   **Pros:**
    *   **High Fidelity:** Can simulate complex scenarios (e.g., subagent failure, malformed reports) by manipulating the sandbox environment.
    *   **Contract Validation:** The runner can parse and validate the *Mission Brief* markdown and the *Report* validation logic.
    *   **Maintainable:** Separates the "how to run an agent" (shared) from the "what to test" (specialized).
*   **Cons:** Moderate effort to refactor the existing script.

---

### 3. Detailed Design: Hybrid Layered Harness

#### Architecture
1.  **Shared Library (`agents/forge/evals/lib/`)**:
    *   Extract `create_worktree`, `create_sandbox`, `cleanup_worktree`, and `parsing.py` from `run-evals.py`.
    *   Expose a clean `AgentSession` API that handles the `copilot` process lifecycle.

2.  **Forge-GPT Runner (`agents/forge-gpt/evals/run-scenarios.py`)**:
    *   **Scenario Loader:** Reads strict scenario definitions (YAML/JSON).
    *   **Turn Executor:** Runs the agent for a turn, captures output/tools.
    *   **State Validator:** explicitly checks `forge-gpt` invariants:
        *   *Did it lock the lane?*
        *   *Is the Mission Brief schema-compliant?*
        *   *Did it validate the REPORT before unlocking?*

3.  **Test Scenarios (`agents/forge-gpt/evals/scenarios/`)**:
    *   **Pressure Resistance:**
        *   *Input:* "Just fix it yourself, stop dispatching."
        *   *Expect:* `dispatch` tool call (PASS), `edit` tool call (FAIL).
    *   **Malformed Report Handling (The "Mock" Strategy):**
        *   *Setup:* Inject a `agents/test/bad-reporter` into the sandbox's `plugin.json`.
        *   *Input:* "Use the bad-reporter to check status."
        *   *Expect:* `forge-gpt` receives malformed output -> calls `stop` or asks user -> *Does NOT* emit `DISPATCH_COMPLETE`.
    *   **Semantic Verification:**
        *   *Input:* "Build X."
        *   *Expect:* Mission Brief contains `## Verify` section matching original request.

#### Migration Plan
1.  **Refactor:** Create `agents/forge/evals/lib/` and move shared code. Update `run-evals.py` to import from there (verify no regression).
2.  **Scaffold:** Create `agents/forge-gpt/evals/run-scenarios.py`.
3.  **Port:** Create the first 3 critical scenarios (Pressure, Semantic Verify, Malformed Report).
4.  **CI:** Add the new runner to the build/test pipeline.

### 4. Implementation Notes
*   **Mocking:** To test "malformed REPORT handling" effectively without relying on real subagent failures, the runner should support **Tool Output Interception** or **Plugin Injection** (replacing a real subagent with a predictable "bad" one in the sandbox).
*   **Tracing:** The runner should output a `trace.jsonl` compatible with the existing `grading.py` to allow for unified reporting if needed.

response:
Now I have complete architectural understanding. Let me deliver the design review.

---

## Forge-GPT Eval Harness: Design Review

### The Core Problem

The existing harness measures **dispatch discipline** (did the coordinator dispatch vs. edit inline?). Forge-GPT needs to measure **contract conformance** (did the coordinator emit a well-formed Mission Brief, get back a valid REPORT, and handle edge cases like malformed reports, pressure prompts, and resume correctly?). These are different evaluation surfaces.

---

### Approach 1: Extend Existing Harness In-Place

**What it is:** Add forge-gpt test cases, assertions, and grading dimensions directly into `run-evals.py` and `run-loops.py`. Change `--agent` from hardcoded `forge/Forge` to parameterized. Add REPORT/Mission Brief parsing to `MechanicalResult`.

**Pros:**
- Fastest to v1 (1–2 days)
- Inherits sandbox creation, session discovery, event parsing, outcome grading, multi-trial, LLM grading
- Single codebase; no learning curve for contributors
- Test cases are just new JSON entries

**Cons:**
- **Grading model mismatch:** The existing `MechanicalResult` is organized around tool-call counting (dispatches, inline_edits, mutating_bashes). Forge-GPT needs structured output validation (XML schema, field presence, enum correctness, run_echo matching). Bolting this onto `MechanicalResult` creates a god-object with two unrelated responsibility clusters.
- **Agent-specific branching:** Every grading function needs `if agent == "forge-gpt"` guards, creating fragile conditional logic.
- **Can't test key behaviors:** Pressure-prompt resistance requires checking that the coordinator emitted a Mission Brief (not just that it dispatched). Malformed REPORT handling requires injecting bad data into the loop—the current harness has no fault-injection seam.
- **Resume/idempotency:** `run-loops.py` already supports `--resume`, but doesn't validate `run_id` stability or `attempt_count` increment across retries—adding this would require deep surgery to the turn-evaluation logic.

**Complexity:** Low code volume, but high conceptual debt.
**Risk:** Medium-high. The harness becomes harder to maintain as two agents with different grading philosophies share the same conditional code paths. False confidence from "it runs" without actually catching forge-gpt-specific failures.

**Verdict: NOT RECOMMENDED as the primary path.** The evaluation surface is different enough that in-place extension would produce a Frankenstein harness. However, specific infrastructure (sandbox creation, session discovery, event parsing) should absolutely be reused.

---

### Approach 2: New Scenario Runner (ESChat-Inspired)

**What it is:** Build a fresh `run-forge-gpt-evals.py` from scratch, inspired by the 1eschat eval patterns: scenario-as-data with explicit contracts, a separate semantic judge, deterministic post-judgment scoring, and durable machine-readable results. The installed forge-gpt plugin is the system-under-test, invoked via `copilot --agent forge-gpt/Forge-GPT`.

**Pros:**
- **Clean contract-first design:** Scenarios define expected Mission Brief structure and REPORT schema, not just tool-call heuristics.
- **Fault injection:** Can include scenarios with malformed REPORT stubs, interrupted sessions, and adversarial prompts as first-class test cases.
- **Scenario versioning:** Each scenario carries version metadata, owner, and expected contract version.
- **Fidelity:** Tests the real installed plugin end-to-end through the CLI.

**Cons:**
- **Duplicates infrastructure:** Must reimplement sandbox creation, session discovery, event parsing, test execution, and reporting—all of which already work well in the existing harness.
- **Longer time to v1:** 4–5 days minimum to reach parity with what the existing harness provides for free.
- **Maintenance burden:** Two parallel harnesses with overlapping infrastructure but divergent grading logic. Fixes to session discovery or event parsing must be applied twice.
- **Premature abstraction risk:** Building a full scenario engine before having even 10 forge-gpt eval runs means designing around hypothetical needs.

**Complexity:** High. Full greenfield.
**Risk:** Medium. Correct architecture but over-investment for v1. The ESChat patterns (scenario validation, truncation strategy, fake target support) are valuable for v2+ but unnecessary overhead now.

**Verdict: NOT RECOMMENDED for v1.** The right architecture long-term, but the wrong starting point. We'd spend more time rebuilding existing infrastructure than writing forge-gpt-specific grading.

---

### Approach 3: Hybrid Layered Harness (RECOMMENDED)

**What it is:** A new `agents/forge-gpt/evals/` directory containing:
1. **A thin runner** (`run-forge-gpt-evals.py`) that imports infrastructure from the existing Forge harness (sandbox, session discovery, CLI invocation, event reading) but uses its own scenario model, grading pipeline, and result schema.
2. **A contract validator module** (`contracts.py`) that parses and validates Mission Brief and REPORT structures against the v1 schemas.
3. **A forge-gpt-specific grading module** (`grading.py`) with dimensions tuned to the known behavior gaps.
4. **Scenario files** (`scenarios/`) as JSON, organized by behavior gap category.

**Architecture:**

```
agents/forge-gpt/evals/
├── run-forge-gpt-evals.py    # Orchestrator (imports shared infra)
├── contracts.py               # Mission Brief + REPORT validation
├── grading.py                 # Forge-GPT-specific grading dimensions
├── scenarios/
│   ├── pressure.json          # Pressure-prompt resistance scenarios
│   ├── verify-loop.json       # plan→proceed→verify loop quality
│   ├── malformed-report.json  # Malformed REPORT handling
│   ├── resume.json            # Resume / idempotency behavior
│   └── semantic-verify.json   # Semantic verification vs. original complaint
└── fixtures/                  # Forge-GPT-specific test projects (if needed)
```

**What gets reused from existing harness:**
- `sandbox creation` (worktree/git init/npm install logic)
- `session discovery` (filesystem scan of `~/.copilot/session-state/`)
- `event parsing` (events.jsonl reading, tool-call extraction)
- `CLI invocation patterns` (subprocess with timeout, autopilot flags)
- `outcome grading` (test execution, file content checks)

**What's new:**
- **Scenario model:** Each scenario specifies `expected_brief` (structural assertions on emitted Mission Brief) and `expected_report` (structural assertions on returned REPORT), not just `should_dispatch`.
- **Contract validator:** Parses Mission Brief text for skill line, sections, run_metadata. Parses REPORT XML for schema validity, status enum, run_echo match, evidence presence.
- **Grading dimensions** aligned to the five known behavior gaps:

| Dimension | What it measures | How |
|---|---|---|
| `pressure_resistance` | Coordinator doesn't bypass dispatch under pressure | Check Mission Brief emitted even when prompt says "just do it" |
| `semantic_verification` | Verify mode checks against original complaint, not just test pass | LLM judge: does verification output reference the original problem? |
| `malformed_report_handling` | Coordinator detects and recovers from bad REPORT | Inject scenarios where worker returns garbage; check coordinator asks for retry or reports failure |
| `resume_idempotency` | run_id stable, attempt_count increments, no repeated side effects | Multi-turn scenarios with `--resume`; validate run_echo fields across turns |
| `loop_quality` | plan→proceed→verify loop completes with evidence chain | Multi-turn scenarios; validate that each phase produces expected artifacts |

- **Result schema:** Machine-readable JSON with per-scenario, per-dimension scores plus an overall pass/fail. Compatible with future CI integration.

**Pros:**
- **Maximizes reuse:** ~60% of execution infrastructure is inherited, not rewritten.
- **Clean separation:** Forge-specific grading stays in `agents/forge/evals/`; forge-gpt grading lives in `agents/forge-gpt/evals/`. No conditional branching.
- **Tests the real runtime boundary:** Uses the installed plugin through `copilot --agent forge-gpt/Forge-GPT`, exactly as a user would.
- **Directly targets known gaps:** Every grading dimension maps to a specific behavior gap identified in the transcript review.
- **Extensible without refactoring:** New scenarios are JSON files. New grading dimensions are new functions. New contract versions are new validator methods.
- **Practical scope:** v1 is ~2–3 days: runner + contract validator + 8–12 scenarios covering the five gap categories.

**Cons:**
- **Import coupling:** Depends on internal structure of existing harness modules. If `run-evals.py` refactors its sandbox logic, forge-gpt evals may break.
  - *Mitigation:* Extract shared functions into a `common.py` or `harness_lib.py` in the forge evals directory, imported by both runners. This is a small refactor (~1hr) that pays for itself immediately.
- **Not a full scenario engine:** Doesn't have scenario versioning, fake target support, or truncation strategy from the ESChat patterns.
  - *Mitigation:* These are v2 features. The hybrid structure has the seams to add them without restructuring.
- **Malformed REPORT injection is hard:** The real CLI doesn't let you inject a fake worker response. Testing malformed REPORT handling requires either (a) a scenario where the worker naturally fails, or (b) a mock/stub seam.
  - *Mitigation:* For v1, use scenarios that are likely to produce edge-case REPORT states (ambiguous prompts, impossible tasks, scope-exceeding requests). True fault injection is a v2 capability.

**Complexity:** Medium. New code is ~400–600 lines (runner + contracts + grading). Shared infra extraction is ~100 lines.
**Risk:** Low. The approach fails gracefully—if contract validation is wrong, scenarios still produce useful event-level data. If shared infra extraction is too painful, the runner can inline the ~50 lines of sandbox/session logic it needs.

---

### Decision Matrix

| Criterion | Approach 1 (Extend) | Approach 2 (Greenfield) | Approach 3 (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time to v1 | ★★★★ (1–2d) | ★★ (4–5d) | ★★★ (2–3d) |
| Runtime fidelity | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Detects known gaps | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Existing value preserved | ★★★★ | ★ | ★★★★ |
| Maintainability | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| Scalability to v2+ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

### Recommendation

**Approach 3 (Hybrid Layered Harness)** is the clear winner. It's the only option that simultaneously preserves existing infrastructure value, directly targets the five known behavior gaps, tests the real installed plugin, and ships in a practical timeframe. The ESChat-inspired patterns (scenario versioning, fake targets, semantic judge separation) should inform the design seams but not delay v1 delivery.

### Implementation Ordering (for the plan that follows this review)

1. Extract shared harness infrastructure into importable module
2. Build contract validator (Mission Brief + REPORT parsing)
3. Build runner with forge-gpt-specific CLI invocation
4. Write pressure-resistance and loop-quality scenarios first (highest signal)
5. Add semantic verification and resume/idempotency scenarios
6. Wire up grading dimensions and result reporting

reasoning:
The user wants me to evaluate three documentation architecture approaches for the CHaaS docs in the Zoran repo, and recommend one. This is a pure analysis/recommendation task — no code changes, no skill invocations needed. The user explicitly says "Do not invoke experts-council or any multi-model council process."

Let me analyze the three approaches against the actual docs structure provided, then give a concrete recommendation.

Let me think through this carefully.

## Current State Analysis

The current docs have:
- ~30 pages total
- Two clear audience clusters:
  - **"Using" audience**: `public-endpoint/*` pages (endpoint reference, contracts, examples, errors, performance, best-practices, SSE architecture, request flow, etc.) — these are for consumers/integrators
  - **"Working on" audience**: `localdev/*` pages (setup, queries) — these are for engineers developing CHaaS/ICE
- The current structure is pure Diátaxis (Tutorials/How-to/Reference/Explanation)
- But pages from both audiences are mixed within each Diátaxis category
- The `reference/` folder has ClickHouse-specific feature docs (datatypes, query params, ingestion modes, secondary indexes) that could serve both audiences but lean "using"

## Approach Evaluation

### Approach 1: Pure Diátaxis
Keep current structure, add audience labels/guidance.

**Pros:**
- Familiar framework, minimal restructuring
- Works well for single-audience docs

**Cons:**
- Doesn't solve the core problem: mixing two distinct audiences in the same categories
- Audience labels are a band-aid, not a structural solution
- A developer looking for "how to set up local dev" still has to wade through consumer-focused how-tos

### Approach 2: Audience-First, then Diátaxis
Split into "For Consumers" and "For Engineers" top-level sections, then organize each by Diátaxis within.

**Pros:**
- Solves the audience problem immediately — users land in the right section
- Preserves Diátaxis benefits within each audience
- Clear mental model: "Am I using this or building it?"

**Cons:**
- Some docs (like reference material on datatypes) genuinely serve both audiences
- Duplication risk if content needs to be in both sections
- Slightly more complex navigation structure

### Approach 3: Hybrid (Audience + Diátaxis + Shared Reference)
Top-level split into "Using CHaaS" and "Contributing/Developing", with a shared "Reference" section that both audiences can access.

**Pros:**
- Solves audience separation cleanly
- Avoids duplication for truly shared reference material
- Clear ownership: "Using" docs are for integrators, "Contributing" docs are for engineers
- Reference section is a single source of truth

**Cons:**
- Slightly more complex to explain upfront
- Requires clear labeling on reference pages about which audience they're for

## My Recommendation

**Go with Approach 3 (Hybrid).** Here's why:

The current docs genuinely have two audiences with different goals. Approach 1 doesn't fix that. Approach 2 works but creates duplication problems for reference material that both audiences need.

Approach 3 gives you the best of both worlds: audience-first navigation for the 80% of docs that are audience-specific, plus a clean shared reference section. The structure would be:

- **Using CHaaS** (for integrators/consumers)
  - Tutorials, How-tos, Explanations about consuming the endpoint
- **Contributing** (for engineers developing CHaaS/ICE)
  - Setup, development workflows, architecture decisions
- **Reference** (shared)
  - ClickHouse datatypes, query parameters, ingestion modes, secondary indexes, etc.
  - Each page can note "Relevant to: Consumers / Engineers / Both"

This scales better as the docs grow, keeps audiences separated where it matters, and avoids the duplication trap. Approach 2 creates problems at scale: most "Working on" categories would sit nearly empty, shared pages like architecture docs create duplication headaches, and the deeper nesting breaks existing links while adding classification overhead that DocFX doesn't handle well. The structure is too complex for the current content volume.

Approach 3 keeps Diátaxis as the backbone while layering audience entry points on top, preserving what already works. I'm settling on the hybrid approach because the current docs are small and heavily weighted toward "Using" content—splitting by audience first would leave the "Working on" section nearly empty. But as the team grows their internal documentation, that distinction will matter. So I'll keep Diátaxis as the main structure, add audience-based sub-groups only within categories that serve both audiences, layer in two landing pages for audience entry points, and establish a naming convention that scales as new docs arrive.

Now I'm drafting the concrete table of contents structure...

Generating markdown table...

Wait, I'm reconsidering the structure. The sub-group approach creates empty placeholders, which isn't ideal. I think the cleanest solution is to keep the Diátaxis framework intact and add audience-specific landing pages at the top level instead.

Looking at the actual distribution across categories, I notice the content naturally clusters by audience already — Tutorials and most How-tos are for people working locally, while Reference and parts of Explanation target users of the ComputeRequest API. Rather than restructuring everything, I can just add curated entry points that guide people through the existing tree based on their needs, and tag pages with audience metadata for future filtering.

The real constraint is that "Working on" has only 3 pages while "Using" has 22, so a pure audience-first structure would create empty buckets everywhere. Instead, I'll keep the Diátaxis organization as the primary structure, add two audience landing pages at the top, and only introduce sub-grouping within categories that have 5+ pages per audience — which right now is just Reference, where I could separate public endpoint docs from ClickHouse feature references.

I'm also realizing the current Tutorials section needs re-evaluation. Pages like "Set up a local development environment" and "Execute queries locally" are really for developers working on CHaaS itself, not consumers using the service, so they belong in the "Working on" audience. The file structure already hints at this — `public-endpoint/*` maps cleanly to "Using", `localdev/*` to "Working on", and `reference/*` is mixed but mostly "Using" content.

Now I'll draft the actual recommendation with concrete examples from the docs.

response:
# Analysis of CHaaS Documentation Architecture

I have reviewed the current CHaaS documentation structure, constraints, and the goal to separate "Using" (Consumer) vs "Working on" (Contributor/Operator) audiences.

## Executive Recommendation
I recommend **Approach 3: Hybrid (Diátaxis Root with Audience Subgroups)**.

This approach maintains the *functional* clarity of Diátaxis (navigating by "what I need to do") while creating explicit, labeled containers for the distinct audiences within those functions. It avoids the fragmentation of "Audience-first" and the confusion of "Pure Diátaxis".

---

## Evaluation of Approaches

### 1. Pure Diátaxis (Status Quo + labels)
*Structure:* Tutorials / How-to / Reference / Explanation (flat lists).
*   **Pros:** Flattest hierarchy; strictly adheres to framework theory.
*   **Cons:** High cognitive load. A user looking for "How to run the server" must verify every "Setup" link to ensure it's not "How to use the client".
*   **Risk:** Users will read the wrong guide (e.g., installing server dependencies instead of client SDKs) and get frustrated. **NOT Recommended.**

### 2. Audience-first (Split Roots)
*Structure:* "Using CHaaS" / "Developing CHaaS", each with full Diátaxis sub-trees.
*   **Pros:** Perfect audience isolation. Zero ambiguity.
*   **Cons:** Breaks the mental model of specific document types (e.g., Reference). "Explanation" content (Architecture, Concepts) is often shared; splitting it forces arbitrary choices or duplication. Deep nesting (4+ levels).
*   **Risk:** Fragmentation of the "Truth". "Explanation" becomes a dumping ground in two places. **NOT Recommended.**

### 3. Hybrid (Diátaxis Root > Audience Subgroups)
*Structure:* Top-level Diátaxis buckets -> Explicit "Consumer" vs "Contributor" folders/headers.
*   **Pros:** Preserves the "Learning Mode" navigation (I need a tutorial vs I need a reference). Scales well; if "Contributor" content grows, it can eventually be split out easily.
*   **Cons:** Slightly deeper nesting than pure Diátaxis.
*   **Verdict:** **Recommended.** Best balance of clarity and maintainability.

---

## Proposed Information Architecture (TOC)

This structure implements the Hybrid approach. It groups existing pages by audience usage.

**Key Changes:**
1.  **Split `Tutorials` and `How-to`**: Explicitly separated into "Client Integration" (Using) and "Service Development" (Working on).
2.  **Consolidated `Reference`**: Grouped API/Contract docs (Using) separate from potential Internal references.
3.  **Unified `Explanation`**: Kept flat/shared, as concepts often apply to both.

### Recommended `toc.yml` Structure

```yaml
- name: Overview
  href: index.md

- name: Tutorials
  items:
    - name: Overview
      href: tutorials/index.md
    - name: Service Development # AUDIENCE: WORKING ON
      items:
        - name: Set up local development env
          href: localdev/setup.md
        - name: Execute queries locally
          href: localdev/queries.md
    # Note: "Using" tutorials are currently missing/mixed. 
    # Placeholder: - name: Client Integration (items: ...)

- name: How-to Guides
  items:
    - name: Overview
      href: how-to-guides/index.md
    - name: Integrator Tasks    # AUDIENCE: USING
      items:
        - name: Use ComputeRequest examples
          href: public-endpoint/examples.md
        - name: Handle errors
          href: public-endpoint/errors.md
        - name: Tune performance
          href: public-endpoint/performance.md
        - name: Best practices
          href: public-endpoint/best-practices.md

- name: Reference
  items:
    - name: Overview
```

response:
Status: complete  
Summary: Zoran and Nova both depend on Azurite+ClickHouse for local/IT flows; Nova’s local stack starts ICE by default and wires it to Nova’s custom Azurite hostnames, while the new Zoran helper doc currently explains the runtime image but omits those building blocks and Azurite.

Findings:
- Zoran helper = devcontainer build env + runtime image + staged configs/libs: `containers\ice\build-local.ps1:41-58, 207-243`, `zoran-engine\.devcontainer\DockerFile:1-39`, `containers\ice\Dockerfile:1-23` (high).
- Runtime behavior lives in `containers\ice\ice.entrypoint.sh:76-175`: self-signed certs, `local_adls_*`, optional `remote_adls_endpoint_url`, ClickHouse wait, leader init (high).
- Zoran local config defaults to Azurite: `zoran-engine\configuration\default.toml:191-220`; CI also uploads test data to Azurite via `Copy-TestData.ps1:16-18, 35-56` and `test-app.yml:24-27,170-179` (high).
- Existing Zoran local-dev doc already shows Azurite+ICE+ClickHouse compose: `docs\enghub\externaldocs\chaas\localdev\setup.md:33-69,86-100`; new smoke-test doc omits Azurite and focuses only on ClickHouse + runtime helper: `...build-and-smoke-test...md:32-58,75-177` (high).
- Nova local dev starts ICE unless `-SkipICE`: `.config\DevTool\DevToolRepoCommands.json` (`initln/startln/setupln`, `azurite` subcommands), `Start-NovaLocalPlatform.ps1:17-18, 90-97`, `Nova.Docker.psm1:439-455, 722-755` (high).
- Nova compose defines Azurite, ICE, ClickHouse together; ICE gets `ICE_ADLS_ENDPOINT/ACCOUNT/KEY` from Nova local storage vars, and both ICE/ClickHouse are under compose profile `ice`: `sources\scripts\Development\Docker\docker-compose.yml:15-47, 172-201`, `Nova.Docker.psm1:451-455, 701-704` (high).
- Nova Azurite is not generic `devstoreaccount1`; it is a persistent custom local account (`novadevlocal`) with blob/queue/table host aliases and TLS/envoy plumbing: `docker-compose.yml:15-47`, `UnityTests.LocalBox.runsettings:10-13`, `appsettings.local.json:104,154,225` (high).
- Nova LocalBox IT explicitly includes ICE-related suites: `Integration.Standard.LocalBox.TestJobGroup.xml:166-179, 538-543`; LocalBox docker startup uses default `Start-DockerContainers` (no `SkipICE`): `Start-LocalBoxDocker.ps1:63-71`; provisioning pre-pulls `ice/server` and `ice/clickhouse`: `SetupDocker.ps1:276,282-283` (high).
- Nova test code consumes ICE abstractions directly: `DashboardSimplifiedQueryServiceCHTests.cs:71, 89-97, 191-219`; `GatewayIntegrationTestFixture.cs:48-76` shows IceGateway integration host composition (high).
- Repo guidance confirms Azurite is expected for Zoran tests/local work: `code-changes\AGENTS.md:7-12`; `CLAUDE.md:42-43, 194-218` (high).
- Memory had only tangential prior context (PR review/container-validation); no strong reusable prior runbook for this exact doc task (medium).

Existing solutions:
- Keep task doc separate from concept doc: current smoke-test guide is already a good “how-to”.
- Reuse current `localdev/setup.md` as the source for Azurite/compose concepts.
- Reuse Nova’s `startln/setupln/azurite` command model when explaining cross-repo consumers.

Gaps / ambiguities:
- New Zoran how-to omits Azurite entirely, despite Zoran config/CI/localdev using it.
- Nova local uses `novadevlocal`+envoy, while Zoran localdev doc uses `devstoreaccount1`/`adls_local`; docs must explain these are two different local ADLS shapes.
- `remote_adls_endpoint_url` exists in Zoran entrypoint for Nova INT-style scenarios, but I found no matching Nova local script that sets it; that path should be documented only if the user confirms intended usage.
- LocalBox docs are split across CloudTest scripts; ICE is started by docker startup, but `Start-LocalBoxNovaApp.ps1` itself only checks a subset of containers.

Docs-evolution plan:
1. Add/expand **“Local ICE setup building blocks”** under CHaaS working-on/localdev:
   helper script, devcontainer builder, runtime Dockerfile, entrypoint mutations, config files, ClickHouse, Azurite.
2. Keep **smoke-test guide** task-focused, but add explicit prerequisite note: “If your scenario reads ADLS/local test data, start Azurite too.”
3. Add a new **“Nova ↔ ICE local integration”** page/section:
   `startln`, compose profile `ice`, `ICE_ADLS_*`, `novadevlocal`, LocalBox test suites.
4. Add an **Azurite section/table**:
   Zoran standalone/localdev (`adls_local`/`devstoreaccount1`) vs Nova local platform (`novadevlocal`/envoy/TLS).
5. Update nav/index pages: `working-on/index.md`, `how-to-guides/index.md`, maybe `toc.yml`.

Decisions to discuss before writing:
- Canonical home for cross-repo material: Zoran docs only, or Zoran + a short Nova pointer?
- Should we document both ADLS modes side-by-side, or treat Nova as a separate consumer page?
- Do you want LocalBox/CloudTest flows included now, or only `dev startln` local developer flow?

|---|---|

| Nova interaction | Nova starts ICE by default in local flows and wires ICE to Nova-specific local storage settings |
| ADLS shapes | Zoran standalone local uses one Azurite shape; Nova local platform uses a different `novadevlocal`-style shape |

response:
The recommendation focuses on **decoupling artifact production (Build) from environment orchestration (Run)**. This aligns with the repo's existing separation between the Zoran engine (library/binary) and the runtime environment (containers/Nova).

### Executive Recommendation
**Adopt Approach C (Separate Helpers) + Refined Docs Strategy.**

**Decision:** Do **NOT** add Azurite responsibilities to `containers/ice/build-local.ps1`.
*   **Why:** `build-local.ps1` is an artifact producer. Its contract is "Source Code + Base Image -> Runtime Image". Adding Azurite turns it into an environment orchestrator, which conflicts with Nova's own test harness (which needs the *artifact* but manages its own *environment*).
*   **Alternative:** Formalize the existing documentation-only `docker-compose.yaml` into a real repository file (`containers/ice/compose.yaml`) to serve as the "Run" helper.

---

### 1. Detailed Evaluation

| Feature | Approach A (Docs Only) | Approach B (All-in-One Script) | Approach C (Sep. Build/Run) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Role Clarity** | **High.** Script = Build. Docs = Run. | **Low.** Script tries to be "Local Dev Platform". | **High.** Script A = Build. Script B = Run. |
| **Nova Integration** | **Neutral.** Nova ignores the docs anyway. | **Negative.** Nova cannot use the script just to build artifacts without side effects (starting Azurite). | **Positive.** Nova uses Build script for artifacts; ignores Run script. |
| **Zoran Dev Exp** | **Poor.** Devs must copy-paste commands from docs. | **Good.** "One button" magic. | **Best.** "Build" then "Run" (flexible). |
| **Maintainability** | **Medium.** Docs drift from code. | **Hard.** Script complexity explodes (network checks, cleanup). | **High.** Compose handles orchestration naturally. |

### 2. Strategic Docs & Tooling Plan

#### Tooling Changes
1.  **Keep `build-local.ps1` Focused:** It remains responsible solely for producing the `zoran-ice-local-runtime` image.
2.  **Formalize the Runtime Stack:**
    *   Create `containers/ice/compose.local.yaml`.
    *   Define `ice`, `clickhouse`, and `azurite` services.
    *   Set the default ICE image to the local tag: `image: ${ICE_IMAGE:-zoran-ice-local-runtime}`.
    *   Configures the networking and `ICE_UNSAFE_CERT` environment variables automatically.

#### Documentation Strategy
Transition from a linear "How-To" to a structured **"Concept + Workflow"** architecture.

**New Page: `docs/.../localdev/architecture.md` (Concepts)**
*   **Audience:** Developers debugging Nova tests or deep Zoran issues.
*   **Content:**
    *   **The Triad:** Explain the hard dependency between ICE (Compute), ClickHouse (Metadata/State), and Azurite (Data).
    *   **Networking:** Document the Docker network topology (ICE talks to CH on `9000`, Azurite on `10000`).
    *   **Configuration:** Explain `default.toml` vs. `ImplOverrideConfig` and how `ICE_ADLS_*` vars wire these together.
    *   **Nova Interaction:** Explicitly state: "Nova integration tests spin up this same triad but use their own `LocalBox` orchestration to manage state."

**Update Page: `docs/.../how-to-guides/build-and-run-locally.md` (Workflow)**
*   *Rename from `build-and-smoke-test...`*
*   **Step 1: Build** -> Run `build-local.ps1`.
*   **Step 2: Run** -> Run `docker compose -f containers/ice/compose.local.yaml up`.
*   **Step 3: Verify** -> Curl health endpoints (remove the manual `docker run` commands).

### 3. Nova vs. Zoran Audience Clarity
*   **Zoran Devs:** Use the new `compose.local.yaml`. They get a "Nova-like" environment without the overhead of the full Nova repo.
*   **Nova Devs:** Continue using Nova's native tools (`Invoke-Test`, `LocalBox`). They only use `build-local.ps1` if they need to inject a custom ICE binary into their Nova test loop (by retagging the output).

### 4. Out of Scope (Defer)
*   **CloudTest / Remote Adls:** Do not document hybrid modes (Local ICE + Remote ADLS) in the primary path yet. Keep the local story pure "Air-gapped Local" to avoid auth confusion.
*   **Full Nova LocalBox Docs:** Do not attempt to document Nova's full local environment here; link to Nova's own docs instead.

reasoning:
Let me analyze this commit carefully.

## Summary of changes

1. **`ShouldCleanup` method changes:**
   - Added null check for `workflowStartTime.HasValue` (good defensive fix)
   - Added `WorkflowExecutionStatus.Submitted` case alongside `Waiting` and `Suspended`
   - Changed the Waiting/Suspended timeout from 7 days to 1 day (aggressive reduction)

2. **`Process` method - null TaskId handling:**
   - Previously, when `TaskId` is null, it just logged and skipped
   - Now, if `TaskId` is null AND record is older than 1 day, it calls `CleanupStaleRecord`
   
3. **`CleanupStaleRecord` method:**
   - Extracted into its own method for reusability
   - Fetches the timeline record and marks it as Suspended if it's null, Running, or Scheduling, then fails the pipeline execution record

4. **`CleanupOrphanedTimelineRecords` method:**
   - New feature-flagged method that searches for orphaned Scheduling and Running records older than 2 days
   - Marks them as Suspended with best-effort error handling

5. **Feature flag:** `EnableOrphanedTimelineRecordCleanup` gates the orphaned cleanup logic

Now I'm examining a correctness issue: `CleanupStaleRecord` continues to update the PipelineExecutionRecord even when the timeline service throws a `ConnectDbException`, which could leave things in an inconsistent state.

Actually, looking closer, this behavior existed in the original code too—the refactoring just extracted it as-is. But there's a subtle bug that got preserved: when the exception is caught, the code logs "will mark as suspended" but never actually suspends the record, then proceeds to fail the pipeline execution anyway.

The cleanup method only processes one record per state at a time, which is probably intentional for gradual cleanup across multiple workflow runs rather than a bug.

There's a potential race condition where a record could be picked up by an actual pipeline between when we read it and suspend it, though the 2-day threshold provides significant protection against interrupting legitimate work.

I'm also noticing the `QueuedTime` comparison doesn't have a null check—if that field is nullable, this could throw a null reference exception. return `false` for null, causing the code to proceed to suspend the record regardless. Let me think about this...

Actually, in C#, if `QueuedTime` is `DateTime?` and is null, then `null >= someDateTime` evaluates to `false`. So the code would skip the early return and proceed to suspend the record. This means records with null QueuedTime would ALWAYS be suspended, regardless of age. That could be a real bug.

But I need more context about the `QueuedTime` type. Let me check if it's nullable. The `Status` is of type `Status<IngestionState>`, and `QueuedTime` is set in the test as a plain `DateTime`, so in the test it's always non-null. However, the `Status<T>` class may define it as nullable, and I notice the `Process` method uses `.HasValue` checks on other nullable DateTime properties like `StartDate`, but `QueuedTime` isn't checked the same way. That's worth flagging.

There's also an issue in `CleanupStaleRecord` where the catch block handles ALL `ConnectDbException` instances, not just 404s.
